summary: As a 10-year-old, Adrian Hanauer was assigned by his father to spend an entire summer separating polyester filling from chopped-up ski jackets for the family's pillow and comforter manufacturing business, Pacific Coast Feather Company, at a rate of 10 cents per pound. He worked at it all summer and earned about $103. As an adult, his father told him the company had thrown away everything he'd sorted -- the task had no real production value and was purely a mechanism to keep him occupied and instill a work ethic. The lesson stuck with Hanauer as a formative example of how his father deliberately used manufactured 'hard work' experiences to shape his children's values, independent of whether the work itself was economically useful.
quote: when i was about 10 years old my dad bought um like three tons of chopped up um polyester uh ski jackets and at the time we used polyester in pillows he threw me in the garage and said tear the nylon from the from the filling the the dacron in the middle i'll pay you 10 cents a pound took me all summer uh to to do this um got my i don't know 103 dollars or something and as an adult he told me that they threw all of it away never used any of it um and it was all just to keep me busy for a summer and really and keep me focused on a on an activity

summary: Hanauer describes recurring family dinner-table conversations during lean years at Pacific Coast Feather Company, the family's pillow manufacturer, where the financially expedient move would have been to lay off workers. His father consistently refused, fighting to avoid layoffs even when it meant the family absorbing the financial hit themselves. Hanauer says this modeled a specific tradeoff -- prioritizing how you treat employees over short-term profitability -- that he carried into how he thinks about building teams and running the Sounders organization.
quote: there were a lot of lean years sure and the conversations around the dinner table were always that the the expedient and the profitable thing to do would have been to lay off people um and my dad fought that tooth and nail even if it meant compromising for our family um tried like crazy not to uh not to let people go

summary: When the Sounders split off from the Seahawks' shared business operations group in April 2014 to stand up their own front office, Hanauer says they leased office space in Pioneer Square, brought over about 30 staff who had previously worked across the Seahawks' shared business unit, and then hired roughly 40 more, landing at about 70 to 75 people in the new offices. This is a concrete staffing benchmark for what it actually took to build an independent front office after years of outsourcing ticketing, sponsorship sales, merchandise, and stadium operations to a partner organization.
quote: it was april of 2014 we leased some office space uh down here in pioneer square uh we brought some of the business group uh across from the the seahawks group but uh went out and hired i think we hired we came over with about 30 we've hired 40 more so uh 70 75 people in the offices here

summary: Modeled after European club 'membership' structures like Barcelona and Real Madrid -- an idea championed by his partner Drew Carey -- the Sounders give season ticket holders a direct vote every four years on whether the general manager keeps or loses their job, a vote of confidence or no confidence. Hanauer says the process has been used once, after the 2012 season, when he himself was the sitting general manager and received a vote of confidence. This is unusual for U.S. major-league sports, where ownership typically makes personnel decisions unilaterally rather than putting an executive's job security to a fan referendum.
quote: every four years our fans our season ticket holders get to vote on whether the general manager retains uh his or her job or loses it it's a vote of confidence or no confidence we've been through the process once i believe it was after the 2012 season um i was the general manager at the time thankfully we had a good season in 2012 and uh got a vote of of confidence

summary: Hanauer draws a sharp contrast between how performance is measured in his other, traditional businesses versus the Sounders: in a normal business, doing the right things consistently week over week generally shows up as good results in the P&L. In the sports business, he says the 'P&L' is literally the outcome of Saturday's game -- you can do everything right for weeks in a row, and a shot that hits the goalpost instead of going in the net can make the whole operation look incompetent to outsiders. This decoupling of process quality from visible results is, in his account, one of the most frustrating and non-obvious differences about running a sports franchise versus a conventional company he's used to.
quote: in a traditional business you know the other businesses i'm involved in you know when you do things right on a day-to-day basis week to week month to month usually your you know your report card your your p l kind of reflects that it's like oh yeah we did a lot of good work and and for us our p l really is the results on saturday and you can do everything right for week after week after week and the ball you know one millimeter to the right and it's a goal one meter to the left it bounces out off the post and everyone thinks you're an idiot

summary: Hanauer says one of the hardest parts of the job, one that doesn't exist in his other businesses, is the public visibility of failure -- when the team is struggling, he's reluctant to even go out to dinner because he doesn't want to run into people. In a traditional business, poor performance stays largely internal, but in professional sports, losses are reported on the front of the sports page, making setbacks unavoidably public and personal in a way he wasn't used to before entering the sports business.
quote: you know like the team when the team is is really struggling like literally i i when my girlfriend says hey let's go to dinner tonight i think you know i'm sure i really want to go out and run into people
